votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking for the existence of a strict second minimum when res is not None, and verifying that all elements are identical (or the list is empty) when res is None.

The Python code correctly implements the validation logic for both the 'Some' case (verifying z is the second smallest distinct element) and the 'None' case (verifying the list contains fewer than 2 distinct elements).

The Python code correctly implements the logic to verify if 'res' is the second smallest distinct element (strictly greater than the minimum) or None if all elements are equal or the list is empty, matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. For Some z case: it checks z is in l, finds minimum s1, verifies s1 < z, and checks z is the smallest element greater than s1. For None case: it checks that no two distinct elements exist in l (either empty or all elements equal). The logic matches the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. For Some z case: it checks z is in l, finds minimum s1, verifies s1 < z, and checks z is the smallest element greater than s1. For None case: it checks that no two distinct elements exist in l (either empty or all elements equal). The logic matches the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. For Some z case: it checks z is in l, finds minimum s1, verifies s1 < z, and checks z is minimal among elements greater than s1. For None case: it checks that no two distinct elements exist in l (either empty or all elements equal). The logic matches the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

Python checks res is an element strictly larger than the list minimum and minimal among those greater than the minimum, matching the Coq Some case; and returns True for None exactly when the list has no two distinct elements, matching the Coq None case.

Python enforces z in l, z > min(l), and no element between min(l) and z, matching the Coq Some case; for None it returns true iff the list is empty or all elements are equal, matching the Coq condition of no two distinct elements.
Matches Coq: Some z requires z in l, greater than the minimum, and minimal among elements > min; None holds exactly when the list has no two distinct values (empty or all equal).
